evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution() has two consecutive Javadoc blocks immediately above it: the first describes both canonical and legacy formats; the second (directly below, without code between them) re-describes only the canonical format — a strong marker of an LLM edit that generated a simplified replacement without removing the original

RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint() has 14 near-identical string-contains checks structured as two parallel blocks (xpos= variants, then tag= variants) with a comment 'Legacy: tag= attribute' — the mechanical duplication of if-contains pattern over all POS groups in each block is a characteristic LLM generation artefact

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern() contains step-marker debug logging: logger.debug('=== SEMANTIC FIELD EXPLORATION ==='), 'Step 1: Finding collocates...', 'Step 2: Finding nouns...', 'Step 3: Scoring...', '--- RESULTS ---' — narrative banners that annotate each step are a common LLM-generated debug pattern

AdjectiveCollocateRanker was 'extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold' per its own Javadoc — this is an LLM-style refactor that splits on a mechanical metric (line count) rather than a domain boundary; AdjectiveCollocateRanker is not independently useful and SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles() is a pass-through facade

GrammarConfigLoader is decomposed into very small private helpers: parseAndValidateVersion() (5 lines, validates one field), validateNoLegacyKeys() (5 lines, checks one key) — micro-extraction characteristic of LLM refactors that aggressively split any multi-step method

PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate() and RelationConfig.mergeLemmaConstraint() are structurally identical implementations — verbatim-duplicate logic across files is a characteristic pattern of LLM generation where the second instance was written from scratch rather than calling the first

ExplorationResult has both a public toMap() and a getEdges() marked @Deprecated with a reference to buildEdges() — the deprecated method is a pass-through wrapper with no logic; this deprecation-wrapper pattern is common in LLM-generated refactors

SemanticFieldExplorer class-level Javadoc contains an extended worked example (house → apartment, building, hotel) that explains the algorithm well — this is genuinely useful, not restating, and is a positive signal against the ai_generated_debt dimension

LogDiceCalculator methods have comments that explain the formula semantics ('Sentinel returned when...', 'Compute relative frequency') — these are useful explanations of non-obvious behaviour, not restatements

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer class-level Javadoc provides a genuine algorithm walkthrough with a concrete example — adds real understanding, not noise
LogDiceCalculator comments explain the UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel distinction from 0.0, which is a non-obvious API contract
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES Javadoc lists valid input values explicitly — useful boundary documentation
CqlUtils method Javadocs include worked examples in @param/@return — appropriate for a utility with non-obvious edge cases
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The AI-generated debt is concentrated in three patterns: duplicate documentation (back-to-back Javadoc blocks), mechanical code duplication (posGroupFromConstraint parallel blocks, duplicate substitution methods), and narrative debug scaffolding (step-banner logging) — all consistent with iterative LLM-assisted development where cleanup passes were not completed.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The most definitive AI artefact is the duplicate Javadoc on buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution — two consecutive blocks with no code between them is an unambiguous editing failure. The posGroupFromConstraint parallel-block structure and the duplicate substitution implementations reinforce a pattern of mechanically replicated code rather than extracted reuse. The debug step-banners are a strong but not unique AI signal. Against this, the codebase avoids pure pass-through wrappers (except the deprecated getEdges()), has useful non-restating comments, and shows clear domain naming throughout. The score is 61: several definitive AI artefacts that would require cleanup, but not pervasive enough to dominate the whole codebase.

issues.summary: buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution() has two consecutive Javadoc blocks — a clear LLM editing artefact
evidence: Two /** ... */ blocks appear in immediate succession, both describing the same private static String buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution(String, String) method with no code between them
The first block documents 'Canonical format' and 'Legacy format (deprecated)' with code examples; the second block documents only 'Canonical format' with a near-identical example
The first block's 'Legacy format' section describes a %s-substitution path that the method body does not contain — the documentation describes code that was removed but not cleaned up
issues.suggestion: Delete the first (longer, now-stale) Javadoc block. Revise the retained block to note that legacy %s patterns are no longer supported and that unrecognized formats throw IllegalArgumentException.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint() duplicates the same pattern-matching logic for xpos= and tag= as two parallel 14-branch if-else blocks
evidence: Lines for xpos: if (s.contains('xpos=\"jj') || s.contains('xpos=jj')) return PosGroup.ADJ; — repeated for VERB, NOUN, ADV, OTHER (x2)
Lines for tag: if (s.contains('tag=\"jj') || s.contains('tag=jj')) return PosGroup.ADJ; — same structure repeated verbatim for all groups
14 individual if-return branches; each xpos branch has a structural twin in the tag block — mechanical replication rather than a data-driven approach
issues.suggestion: Replace with a small lookup table: Map<String, PosGroup> prefixToGroup = Map.of("jj", PosGroup.ADJ, "vb", PosGroup.VERB, ...) and iterate over ["xpos", "tag"] attributes × prefix entries. This reduces 14 branches to ~8 lines and makes adding new POS groups a single-entry change.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern() uses LLM-style step-marker debug banners ('=== STEP N ===') woven through business logic
evidence: logger.debug('\n=== SEMANTIC FIELD EXPLORATION ==='), logger.debug('Step 1: Finding collocates for...',seed), logger.debug('Step 2: Finding nouns for each collocate...'), logger.debug('Step 3: Scoring nouns by shared {}...',relationName), logger.debug('\n--- RESULTS ---') — narrative scaffolding that traces execution rather than logging meaningful diagnostic state
Per-item lambda logging (seedRelations.forEach(a -> logger.debug('    {} (logDice={})',...))) executes the lambda even when debug is disabled — no isDebugEnabled() guard
issues.suggestion: Remove the step-banner strings. Replace per-item forEach logging with a single summarising log call: logger.debug('exploreByPattern seed={} steps: collocates={} candidateNouns={} discovered={}', seed, seedRelations.size(), nounProfiles.size(), discoveredNouns.size()); and guard with if (logger.isDebugEnabled()).

issues.summary: AdjectiveCollocateRanker was extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer solely to keep the parent class under 400 lines — a mechanical split with no domain boundary
evidence: AdjectiveCollocateRanker Javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold.'

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles() is documented as 'an intentional facade over AdjectiveCollocateRanker so that callers need only depend on SemanticFieldExplorer' — the facade re-exposes all three arguments unchanged
AdjectiveCollocateRanker has no independent callers and is only instantiated in SemanticFieldExplorer's constructor
issues.suggestion: Inline AdjectiveCollocateRanker back into SemanticFieldExplorer (the original class is now smaller than 400 lines with other refactors applied) or rename it to reflect a genuine responsibility — CollocateProfileBuilder or SeedComparisonEngine — that would justify its independent existence.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.getEdges() and ComparisonResult.getEdges() are deprecated pass-through wrappers with no logic, a residual of LLM-style incremental renaming
evidence: ExplorationResult.getEdges(): @Deprecated public List<Edge> getEdges() { return buildEdges(); } — body is a single forwarding call
ComparisonResult.getEdges(): identical pattern
There are no call sites for getEdges() in the production codebase (grep confirms only buildEdges() is called) — the deprecated wrapper exists for nothing
issues.suggestion: Delete getEdges() from both ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult. There are no remaining callers, so removal is safe.

root_causes: Iterative LLM-assisted development where multiple revision sessions each added or reorganised code without a cleanup pass to remove superseded documentation and methods
Line-count-driven refactoring heuristic applied mechanically (AdjectiveCollocateRanker extraction) rather than informed by domain cohesion
